coredata.dc:title: Spectrofluorometric determination of calcium in the human nasal secretions investigating the association with olfactory function
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelazim, Ahmed H.
Alaqel, Saleh l.
Almalki, Atiah H.
Alharbi, Adnan
Algarni, Majed A.
Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Abdelazim, Mohamed H.
coredata.dc:description: Ionic microenvironment of the nasal secretions especially calcium ions play essential role in the olfactory transmission. However, there is a critical need to determine the free calcium levels in healthy people's nasal secretions in contrast to those of patients with olfactory impairment. A selective spectrofluorometric method was created to quantify nasal calcium levels utilizing its quenching ability to the fluorescence of the functionalized carbon quantum dots. The surface of carbon quantum dots was functionalized with calcium ionophore A23187 and ion association complex, calcium phosphotungstate, to improve the selectively to quantify calcium ions. The functionalized carbon quantum dots exhibited a concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching upon interaction with calcium ions. Different factors influencing the quenching process were done to provide efficient analytical process. The new method, demonstrated accurate calcium determination over the concentration range of 200–4000 ng/mL. The suggested technique was used to measure the calcium in the nasal secretions of both healthy people and patients with olfactory impairment. The findings revealed significantly higher calcium levels in the patient with olfactory dysfunction (healthy vs. patient; 735 ± 20 ng/mL vs. 2987 ± 37 ng/mL, p < 0.05).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quenching
CQDs
Calcium
Nasal secretions
Olfactory dysfunction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ion association complex of calcium
Functionalization of the CQDs with calcium recognizing materials
Functionalization of CQDs with calcium recognizing materials
Measurement of calcium levels in the nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and individuals with olfactory impairment
A comparative analysis of the suggested approach and the previously documented spectrofluorometric approach

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of non-functionalized and functionalized CQDs.
Fluorescence spectrum, excitation and emission of the functionalized CQDs.
Quenching of the fluorescence of the functionalized CQDs upon presence of different concentrations of calcium.
Graphical representation for factors affecting the proposed reactions.
Method performance and validation data obtained with the proposed method.
Calcium determination in spiked nasal secretions.
Determination of calcium in presence of different interfering compounds.
Measured calcium in the nasal secretions of healthy individuals and patients with olfactory dysfunction.
Comparative analysis of the suggested approach and the previously documented spectrofluorometric approach.

row.entry: Quenching for the fluorescence of the functionalized carbon quantum dots
Spectrofluorlmeter integrated with flow injection system
More selective by using calcium recognizing materials
Time-consuming, takes several hours to prepare and analyze samples

head.ce:title: Spectrofluorometric determination of calcium in the human nasal secretions investigating the association with olfactory function

ce:affiliation.ce:label: h
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Otolaryngology, Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Damietta 34518, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Otolaryngology, Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, 34518, Damietta, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ionic microenvironment of the nasal secretions especially calcium ions play essential role in the olfactory transmission. However, there is a critical need to determine the free calcium levels in healthy people's nasal secretions in contrast to those of patients with olfactory impairment. A selective spectrofluorometric method was created to quantify nasal calcium levels utilizing its quenching ability to the fluorescence of the functionalized carbon quantum dots. The surface of carbon quantum dots was functionalized with calcium ionophore A23187 and ion association complex, calcium phosphotungstate, to improve the selectively to quantify calcium ions. The functionalized carbon quantum dots exhibited a concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching upon interaction with calcium ions. Different factors influencing the quenching process were done to provide efficient analytical process. The new method, demonstrated accurate calcium determination over the concentration range of 200–4000 ng/mL. The suggested technique was used to measure the calcium in the nasal secretions of both healthy people and patients with olfactory impairment. The findings revealed significantly higher calcium levels in the patient with olfactory dysfunction (healthy vs. patient; 735 ± 20 ng/mL vs. 2987 ± 37 ng/mL, p < 0.05).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ionic microenvironment of the nasal secretions has a crucial part in the olfactory cleft's chemical-electrical transduction pathway. They aid in the transmission of smell from the nasal lumen to the central nervous system [1]. Calcium ions play a major role in the downstream signalling pathways of olfactory receptors, which include regulatory feedback mechanisms. Two different pathways are thought to be involved in the development of calcium's inhibitory actions. First, calcium-calmodulin interacts directly with channels that are regulated by cyclic nucleotides. This decreases the channel's receptiveness to cyclic nucleotides, which in turn reduces the amount of positive current that enters the channel. The second pathway has to do with calcium-induced phosphorylation and the subsequent inhibition of adenylyl cyclase. As a result, the activity of cyclic nucleotide-gated channels is decreased by a decrease in intracellular cyclic adenosine monophosphate. The latter of these systems may regulate the regulation of the adaptation of the olfactory response to extended exposure to stimuli. To put it another way, an increase in mucosal calcium levels may cause the olfactory receptor neurons to become less sensitive. Thus, it is postulated that minute changes in mucosal calcium levels could significantly affect the sensitivity of cyclic nucleotide-gated channels, which in turn could affect the excitability of receptor neurons in vivo and improve the sense of smell [2–6].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), nanoscale carbon-based materials, hold promising approach for advancing quantitative analysis techniques due to their unique optical and strong fluorescence characters. The ability of CQDs to undergo quenching presents opportunities for their utilization in the spectrofluorometric determination of various compounds where the determination at trace levels is recommended [7–9]. One of the main challenge to analysts is to improve the selectivity of CQDs through modifying their surface characters. Functionalization of CQDs with calcium recognizing materials can improve the quantification of calcium ions with specific impact on the sensitive and selective detection approaches.

Despite the acknowledged involvement of calcium in nasal secretion and olfactory function, there is currently no validated quantification method that shows the levels of free calcium in healthy individuals' nasal secretions compared to those of patients with olfactory impairment. Thus, the objective of this work is to develop a selective spectrofluorometric method to determine nasal calcium levels through utilizing the quenching ability of the carbon quantum dots that functionalized with calcium ionophore and calcium phosphotungstate. In addition, this work aims to investigate the concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching of the functionalized carbon quantum dots upon binding with calcium with optimizing various factors influencing this quenching process. The developed method is capable of accurately determining calcium within the range of 200–4000 ng/mL. Furthermore, the developed method is applied to determine calcium levels in nasal secretions from both healthy individuals and patients with olfactory impairment and the findings revealed significantly higher calcium levels in the patients with olfactory dysfunction.
Calcium chloride, ethanol (El Nasr Co., Egypt). Carbon quantum dots blue (CQDs), (99.99 %), (Nanoshel., India). Calcium ionophore A23187, phosphotungstic acid (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany).
Jasco FP -6200 Spectrofluorometer (Japan).
Freshly calcium chloride solutions (100 μg/mL) was made in borate buffer solution, pH (9.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ion association complex of calcium
ce:section.ce:para: Ion association complex, namely calciumconten

phosphotungstate (Ca-PT), were created. 100 mL of phosphotungstic acid (10−2 M) was combined with 50 mL of calcium solution (10−2 M). The formed precipitates were filtered, washed, dried, and ground to fine powder. It was finally prepared in ethanol solution (10 mM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of the CQDs with calcium recognizing materials
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mL of 20 mM CQDs was mixed with 2 mL of 10 mM Ca-PT in a 10-mL volumetric flask, which was subjected to reflux at 60 °C for 2 h. Following this, 1 mL of dichloromethane was added, and the total volume was adjusted to 10 mL using borate buffer at pH 9.0. After filtering, the mixture was dialyzed for 2 h against deionized water. For advised usage, the resultant solution was kept at 4 °C. Following excitation at 390 nm, the functionalized CQDs' fluorescence intensity was measured at an emission wavelength of 480 nm.

In order to produce the solvent calibration curve, 10-mL volumetric flasks prefilled with 0.5 mL of the functionalized CQDs solution and 1 mL borate buffer solution, pH 9, were used to prepare a series of calcium standard solutions ranging from 2 to 40 μg. After thoroughly combining the solutions, they were let to stand for 10 min at room temperature. The same parameters were used to prepare a blank sample. Following excitation at 390 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the treated calcium solutions (F) and a blank sample (F0) were measured at 480 nm. The process of creating the calibration curve involved graphing (F0/F) versus calcium solutions in ng/mL.

On the other hand, a matrix-matched calibration curve was established in which tubes holding 1 mL of human nasal secretion were filled with quality control calcium samples. These samples included the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) at 115 ng/mL, lower quantifiable concentration (LQC) at 200 ng/mL, middle quantifiable concentration (MQC) at 2000 ng/mL, and high quantifiable concentration (HQC) at 4000 ng/mL. After that, 3 mL of acetonitrile was added to the samples, and they were centrifuged to get rid of any remaining protein and cell debris. After being dried, the resultant supernatants were reconstituted in 1 mL of pH 9 borate buffer solution and thoroughly mixed. The prepared samples were then treated as earlier described in the solvent calibration to construct the matrix-matched calibration curve. The matrix effect was evaluated using the following equation:
%
Matrix
effect =
S l o p e
m a t r i x
c a l i b r a t i o n
S l o p e
s o l v e n t
c a l i b r a t i o n
.
100

Two participant groups were given 4 mL of sterile borate buffer solution with a pH of 9 in their noses to gather samples of their nasal secretions. There were two groups of patients: the first group included 55 healthy persons, while the second group included 65 patients who showed evidence of olfactory impairment. Clinically, the patients' olfactory dysfunction was categorized into four groups: idiopathic reasons (7 patients), post-trauma (8 patients), Sino-nasal abnormalities (20 patients), and COVID-19 (30 patients). The expelled secretions were collected in a plastic petri dish and gently mixed with 3 mL acetonitrile to induce protein denaturation in the samples. After being moved to designated tubes, the samples were centrifuged for 35 min at 4000 rpm after being mixed for a minute on a vortex mixer. Using the standard addition techniques, which involve adding specified quantities of a recognized standard calcium solution to samples of nasal secretions, the calcium content of the nasal secretions was determined. For each individual, the measured fluorescence intensity was utilized to determine their calcium content. In general, all subjects provided their informed consent, and the Medicine's ethics committee at Al-Azhar University, Egypt approved the study. The described procedures were done in accordance with relevant guidelines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of CQDs with calcium recognizing materials
ce:section.ce:para: Functionalizing CQDs with calcium ionophore A23187 and ion association complex, Ca-PT, alter the CQDs' surface and enhance the ability to measure calcium ions more precisely. It was accomplished by covalent bonding, in which the functional groups in the calcium ionophore and the reactive functional groups on the surface of CQDs interact. FTIR spectroscopy, was applied to investigate the functionalized CQDs as exhibited in Fig. 1 . The observed peaks indicated the surface functionalization of the CQDs. The spectra showed a large, sharp peak at 3440 cm−1, which pointed to the CQDs' surface-activated hydroxyl groups (-OH). A different peak (2894 cm−1) was associated with an amino group (N–H). The presence of carboxylic acid groups was suggested by the prominent peaks at 1700 cm−1, which showed the presence of carbonyl groups (CO). Additionally, the presence of aromatic carbon-carbon double bonds (CC) was revealed by the peak at 1630 cm−1. The carbon-oxygen (C–O) bond stretching vibrations were represented by peaks at 1043 cm−1 and 1081 cm−1, which indicated the CQDs' optical characteristics [10–18].

The functionalized CQDs exhibited fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm when stimulated at 391 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 . This emission of fluorescence is caused by excited electrons and holes recombining inside the CQD structure [17,18].

Through collisional contact between the excited state of the functionalized CQDs and calcium, it develops dynamic quenching of the fluorescence of the CQDs by calcium. The ability of calcium to develop quenching of the dots arises from different processes, including excited state deactivation, and energy transfer between calcium and CQDs. By functionalizing and activating the surface of the produced CQDs, it interacts efficiently with the functional groups of calcium selectively recognizing materials was enhanced. The functionalized surface of the CQDs binds to calcium, which lowers the CQDs' fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity of the functionalized CQDs gradually decreased with increasing calcium concentration as exhibited in Fig. 3 .

The conditions required for efficient analysis process were examined. The effect of pH on the quenching process was evaluated by testing the fluorescence quenching at different pH levels (3−10) both with and without the addition of buffer solutions. As seen in Fig. 4 a, effective quenching was achieved with a pH (9) borate buffer solution. It is clear that the quenching response exhibited a significant decrease with increasing acidity of the solution. Several volumes were examined to determine the ideal volume of the buffer solution, and the findings showed that 1 mL produced the best results (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, it was confirmed how different CQDs solution volumes affected the quenching of the fluorescence. The results showed that 0.5 mL of the CQDs solution gave the optimum fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the study examined fluorescence quenching kinetics at various time intervals (0–30 min). Findings indicate that maximum quenching of CQDs occurred within 2 min, suggesting a rapid interaction with calcium. Prolonged reaction times did not result in additional quenching, as illustrated in Fig. 4d.

The validation parameters of the method were assessed following the ICH M10 guidelines [19]. Table 1 provides a summary of the results obtained for linearity, accuracy, and precision. It was determined that the method exhibited linearity within the concentration range of 200–4000 ng/mL. At 115 ng/mL, the low limit of quantification (LLOQ) of the method demonstrated significant sensitivity. Calibration curves with solvent and matrix-matched standards were established as part of the matrix impact evaluation process. Their slope ratio of 102 % suggested that the matrix influence on method precision was insignificant. Replica analyses of low-, medium-, and high-quality control samples conducted on the same and separate days were used to evaluate accuracy and precision and show adherence to established values (Table 1).

Small changes were made to parameters that might affect the outcome in order to evaluate the method's robustness. Table 1 shows the adjustments made to the buffer pH (9 ± 0.5), buffer volume (1 ± 0.25 mL), CQD volume (0.5 ± 0.1 mL), and incubation duration (2 ± 1 min), along with an assessment of the method's effects. The outcomes showed that the performance of the approach was not significantly impacted by these little parameter changes. In order to confirm the method's suitability for use in clinical settings, it was also tested to ascertain the calcium concentration in samples of spiked nasal secretions. The findings, which are shown in Table 2 , demonstrated that the nasal matrix had no impact on the measurements, confirming the specificity of the processes used and the method's dependability for clinical applications.

To assess the impact of different interfering ions on calcium determination using the described method, spiking calcium with different interfering ions, including sodium, potassium and magnesium, was done. These ions were selected as they are mostly relevant in the nasal secretions. The results were presented in Table 3 where the mean recovery percentage ± RSD of calculated calcium was found to be 99.70 % ± 0.61. This indicates that the proposed method is selective when used to determine calcium in complex samples containing these interfering compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of calcium levels in the nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and individuals with olfactory impairment
ce:section.ce:para: The content of calcium in the nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and patients with olfactory impairment and healthy volunteers was measured using the established spectrofluorometric approach. The findings, Table 4 , revealed that both groups had calcium in the nasal secretions, with remarked higher levels in patients with olfactory dysfunction (healthy vs. patient; 735 ± 20 ng/mL vs. 2987 ± 37 ng/mL, p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: A comparative analysis of the suggested approach and the previously documented spectrofluorometric approach
ce:section.ce:para: The comprehensive analysis in Table 5 demonstrates the benefits of the proposed method in terms of simplicity, selectivity, and efficiency compared to the previously reported approach [20]. The proposed method employs simple and direct measurement for the quenching efficiency of the fluorescent functionalized carbon quantum dots, offering selective calcium determination. In contrast, the reported method [20] relies on a reaction with calcem and necessitates a spectrofluorimeter integrated with a flow injection system. The propose method demonstrates higher sensitive features quantifying calcium in the linearity range from 200 to 4000 ng/mL, in comparison to less sensitive linear range of the reported method at 3000–7000 ng/mL. In terms of selectivity, the proposed method excels by using calcium-recognizing materials.

Functionalized carbon quantum dots were used to establish a spectrofluorometric method for measuring the calcium content of nasal mucus. High selectivity for calcium-sensitive determination was demonstrated by the produced dots. Calcium levels in nasal secretions could be measured thanks to the concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching that occurred from the interaction between the dots and calcium. The technique showed good precision, accuracy, and linearity. When the nasal secretions of olfactory dysfunction patients and healthy people were compared, it was found that the patient with olfactory impairment had far greater levels of calcium.
This research was funded by Taif University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (TU-DSPP-2024-175).

Ahmed H. Abdelazim: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology. Saleh l. Alaqel: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Atiah H. Almalki: Funding acquisition, Investigation. Adnan Alharbi: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Majed A. Algarni: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Maram H. Abduljabbar: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed H. Abdelazim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relations that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-175).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrofluorimetric flow-injection determination of calcium using Calcein
